title: Protecting Routes
intro: Your task manager has login working, but a curious user discovers they can access other people's tasks just by changing the URL. Time to lock down your routes.

teach.title: Route Protection 101
teach.body: Protected routes are pages or API endpoints that require authentication. On the frontend, you redirect unauthenticated users to login. On the backend, middleware checks for a valid session or token before processing any request. Both layers are needed — frontend guards improve UX, but backend checks enforce real security.

teach.title: The Two Layers of Route Protection
teach.code:
Frontend: User visits /dashboard → Check if logged in → If not, redirect to /login

Backend: API receives GET /api/tasks → Middleware checks auth token → If invalid, return 401
                                      → If valid, only return THAT user's tasks
teach.annotation: Frontend protection is for UX (redirecting). Backend protection is for security (blocking). Never rely on frontend alone.

teach.body: Always enforce authorization on the server. Frontend route guards are a convenience for users, not a security measure. Every API endpoint must verify who is making the request.

start.narrative: A user named Alex discovers that by visiting /api/tasks?userId=42 they can see another user's tasks. Your API returns tasks based on the userId query parameter without checking who's logged in. How do you respond?

choices.text: Hide the userId parameter from the frontend URL so users can't see it
Add middleware that extracts the user ID from the auth token instead of the query parameter
Add a frontend check that prevents navigating to other users' pages

hide-param.narrative: Hiding the parameter doesn't help — anyone with browser dev tools can see the API calls and change the userId. Within hours, someone on Twitter posts about the vulnerability.

choices.text: Okay, implement proper server-side auth checks now
Add rate limiting to slow down attackers

fix-middleware.narrative: You add auth middleware that reads the user ID from the verified JWT token, not the query parameter. Now GET /api/tasks always returns the logged-in user's tasks. But you notice the PUT /api/tasks/:id endpoint still doesn't check if the task belongs to the current user. What do you do?

choices.text: Audit all API endpoints and add ownership checks to every one that accesses user data
Just fix the PUT endpoint and move on

frontend-only.narrative: The frontend check works in the browser, but a user uses curl to call your API directly, bypassing the frontend entirely. They can still access any user's data.

choices.text: Implement server-side middleware to validate auth on every API request
Block curl requests by checking the User-Agent header

full-audit.narrative: You audit all 12 API endpoints, add ownership checks to each one, and write tests to verify unauthorized access returns 403. The vulnerability is fully patched.
full-audit.feedback: This is the right approach. One vulnerable endpoint usually means others are too. A full audit with tests ensures you catch everything and prevents regressions.

partial-fix-end.narrative: The PUT endpoint is fixed, but the DELETE endpoint still lets users delete each other's tasks. A week later, someone exploits it and users lose data.
partial-fix-end.feedback: Fixing one endpoint at a time is whack-a-mole. When you find an authorization bug, audit all endpoints systematically — the same pattern likely repeats.

band-aid-end.narrative: Your band-aid solution is easily bypassed. The core problem — missing server-side authorization — remains. User data continues to leak.
band-aid-end.feedback: Security through obscurity (hiding params, blocking user agents) never works. Real security requires the server to verify identity and enforce data ownership on every request.
